sections.title: Description
sections.content: Wife and sister of Zeus, Hera rules as Queen of Olympus and protector of marriage, women, and childbirth. Though her mythology is dominated by Zeus's infidelities and her vengeful responses, she represents the sacred institution of marriage and the sovereignty of divine queenship.

description: Wife and sister of Zeus, Hera rules as Queen of Olympus and protector of marriage, women, and childbirth. Though her mythology is dominated by Zeus's infidelities and her vengeful responses, she represents the sacred institution of marriage and the sovereignty of divine queenship.

name: Hera
subtitle: Queen of the Gods, Goddess of Marriage and Family
summary: Wife and sister of Zeus, Hera rules as Queen of Olympus and protector of marriage, women, and childbirth. Though her mythology is dominated by Zeus's infidelities and her vengeful responses, she represents the sacred institution of marriage and the sovereignty of divine queenship.

worshipPractices.name: Libations
worshipPractices.description: Libations, the pouring of liquids as offerings, were a common form of worship to Hera. These offerings, often wine or milk, were made at her altars to seek her favor and blessings, especially concerning marriage and family matters. The act symbolized respect and supplication to the queen of the gods.

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifices
worshipPractices.description: Animal sacrifices, particularly cows and heifers, were performed in Hera's honor at her temples. The sacrifice was a way to show devotion and ask for her intervention in matters of marriage, childbirth, and the general well-being of women. The meat was often shared in a communal feast after the sacrifice.

worshipPractices.name: Votive Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Devotees would dedicate votive offerings, such as statues, jewelry, and inscribed tablets, to Hera at her sanctuaries. These offerings served as a tangible expression of gratitude for answered prayers or as a plea for future blessings related to marriage, fertility, and family happiness. The objects reflected the donor's wealth and piety.

festivals.name: Heraia
festivals.description: The Heraia was a festival held in Argos, Hera's principal sanctuary, featuring athletic competitions for unmarried women. The victors were crowned with olive wreaths and awarded portions of a sacrificial cow. The festival celebrated female strength and devotion to Hera.

festivals.name: Theogamia
festivals.timing: Various dates, typically associated with local weddings
festivals.description: The Theogamia, meaning 'divine marriage,' was a festival celebrating the sacred marriage of Zeus and Hera. These celebrations often involved rituals that mirrored the wedding ceremony and reaffirmed the sanctity of marriage within the community. It reinforced the social order and celebrated fertility.

cultural.modernLegacy: Hera's image as a powerful queen and symbol of marriage continues to resonate in modern art, literature, and film. Her name and attributes are sometimes invoked in discussions about female empowerment and the complexities of marriage and relationships. The enduring narrative of Hera's struggles also prompts contemporary reflections on themes of jealousy, betrayal, and resilience.

associations.name: Peacock
associations.significance: The peacock's elaborate plumage symbolizes Hera's regal beauty and pride as Queen of the Gods.

associations.name: Lily
associations.significance: Lilies represent purity, rebirth, and motherhood, connecting to Hera's role as protector of women and childbirth.

associations.name: Air
associations.significance: Air symbolizes Hera's all-encompassing presence and authority as Queen of Olympus, overseeing all the realms.

associations.name: Purple
associations.significance: Purple represents royalty, power, and sovereignty, reflecting Hera's status as Queen of the Gods.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven is considered a sacred and complete number, aligning with Hera's role in overseeing the entirety of marriage and family.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The moon is linked to femininity and cycles, mirroring Hera's association with women, childbirth, and the rhythms of life.

associations.name: Marriage
associations.significance: Hera is the protector of marriage, making it a core symbol of her dominion and influence.

companions.name: Iris
companions.description: Iris is Hera's personal messenger, often tasked with delivering messages and carrying out Hera's commands, especially those involving vengeance.

companions.name: Argus Panoptes
companions.description: Argus was a giant with a hundred eyes, serving Hera as a watchman. He was tasked with guarding Io, one of Zeus's lovers, to prevent Zeus from visiting her.

companions.name: Hebe
companions.description: Hebe served as Hera's cupbearer, offering ambrosia and nectar to the gods, before she married Heracles.

companions.name: Peitho
companions.description: Peitho is the goddess of persuasion and seduction, who sometimes accompanies Hera, assisting her in manipulating situations to her advantage.

companions.name: Ceryneian Hind
companions.description: The Ceryneian Hind was a mythical deer with golden antlers sacred to Artemis and associated with Hera, showcasing her connection to powerful creatures.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content:
Hera's origins are deeply rooted in the primordial forces of the cosmos, predating even the Olympian pantheon. As a daughter of Cronus and Rhea, she was, like her siblings, swallowed whole by her father, fearing a prophecy that one of his children would overthrow him. This act highlights the power dynamics inherent in early Greek mythology and the struggle for dominance. Eventually, Zeus, with the help of Metis and Gaia, tricked Cronus into vomiting up his offspring, including Hera. 

The account of her birth varies. Some versions place her birth on the island of Samos, a location that would become closely associated with her worship. Others suggest she was born in Argos or even Arcadia, highlighting the widespread nature of her influence across ancient Greece. The act of being swallowed and regurgitated symbolizes a cycle of death and rebirth, linking Hera to the earth's regenerative powers and the continuity of generations. 

While her parents were Titans, Hera's inclusion in the Olympian pantheon signifies a shift in power and a new order established under Zeus's rule. This transition marks a departure from the older, more chaotic forces of the Titans to a more structured and hierarchical divine society. Despite her subordinate role to Zeus, Hera's inherent power and her role as his wife underscore her importance in maintaining cosmic balance and order.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Temples
extendedContent.content:
Hera was widely venerated throughout ancient Greece, with numerous temples and sanctuaries dedicated to her honor. Argos was considered her primary cult center, boasting the Heraion of Argos, one of the most ancient and significant temples dedicated to her. Pausanias, in his *Description of Greece*, provides detailed accounts of the temple and its surrounding area, describing the impressive statue of Hera crafted by Polyclitus. Other important cult sites included Samos, where the Heraia festival was celebrated, and Olympia, where she shared a temple with Zeus.

Hera's worship was deeply intertwined with the cycles of life, particularly marriage and childbirth. Sacrifices were offered to her to ensure marital harmony, fertility, and safe delivery. The *Hieros Gamos*, or sacred marriage, a ritual reenactment of the union between Hera and Zeus, was a central element of her cult, symbolizing the sanctity of marriage and the divine blessing bestowed upon it. This ritual often involved processions, feasts, and symbolic acts of union, reinforcing the social and religious significance of marriage in ancient Greek society.

The festivals dedicated to Hera, such as the Heraia games held in Olympia for women, showcased the importance of female athleticism and civic participation in her cult. These games, predating the male-only Olympic Games, demonstrate Hera's association with female strength, endurance, and communal identity. The widespread distribution of her temples and the diverse rituals associated with her worship reflect the multifaceted nature of her role as queen of the gods and protector of women.

extendedContent.title: Iconography & Representation
extendedContent.content:
Hera's iconography consistently emphasizes her status as queen and her association with marriage and fertility. She is typically depicted as a mature, dignified woman, often adorned with a crown or diadem, symbols of her royal authority. Her garments are usually rich and flowing, reflecting her wealth and power. The scepter, another common attribute, reinforces her role as ruler of Olympus alongside Zeus. 

Animals sacred to Hera, such as the peacock, cow, and lion, frequently appear in her iconography. The peacock, with its iridescent plumage, symbolizes beauty, pride, and immortality, reflecting Hera's own regal bearing and eternal status. The cow, a symbol of fertility and motherhood, underscores her role as protector of childbirth and women. The lion, a symbol of strength and sovereignty, further emphasizes her power and authority as queen of the gods. 

Artists often portrayed Hera in scenes depicting her marriage to Zeus or her interactions with other deities. These representations frequently showcase her beauty, grace, and unwavering sense of justice. While her vengeful nature is also a prominent aspect of her mythology, her iconography often focuses on her more positive attributes, such as her role as a protector of marriage and family. These visual representations served to reinforce her position as a powerful and respected figure in the Olympian pantheon.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
Hera's role in the cosmic order is complex and often paradoxical. As the wife of Zeus and Queen of Olympus, she holds a position of immense power and influence, responsible for upholding the sanctity of marriage and family. Her presence ensures the continuity of generations and the stability of the Olympian pantheon. However, her mythology is also marked by her relentless pursuit of revenge against Zeus's lovers and illegitimate children, often disrupting the cosmic harmony she is meant to maintain.

Her actions, while often motivated by jealousy and anger, can also be interpreted as a defense of the sacred institution of marriage and the authority of the queen. She represents the consequences of infidelity and the importance of maintaining marital fidelity, even among the gods. Her wrath serves as a deterrent to those who would violate the sacred bonds of marriage and family, reinforcing the social and moral order of ancient Greek society. 

Despite her sometimes destructive tendencies, Hera's presence is essential for maintaining balance in the Olympian world. Her unwavering dedication to her role as queen and her commitment to upholding the sanctity of marriage ensure the continuation of divine rule and the stability of the cosmos. Her struggles and triumphs reflect the complexities of power, love, and justice in the ancient Greek worldview.
